ground_truth.support_text: Quote: "We randomly allocated the manuscripts accepted for review into four groups defined by the interventions"; "Then, manuscripts were randomly allocated (by AS) using a computer program that first stratifies by study type, and second allocates to intervention groups while minimizing differences in initial quality. Insufficient reporting of the allocation procedure.

ground_truth.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel: All outcomes
ground_truth.support_text: Unclear if the editorial team was blinded. Apart from half of them receiving the intervention, the clinical reviewers seem to be unaware of the study objective and randomization. Unclear whether it is possible for a reviewer to be in multiple intervention groups, based on randomization of manuscripts and the possibility for receiving several manuscripts over the trial period.

ground_truth.domain: Blinding of outcome assessors: All outcomes
ground_truth.support_text:
Quote: "Two evaluators (EC, RD) independently rated the reporting quality of manuscripts at initial submission and following peer review and revision, according to the MQAI. Both knew the initial and final status but were blinded to the intervention group."; "On the use of a checklist, the evaluators were able to guess the intervention group in 65.3% (95% CI from 53.5 to 76.0%) of the 75 over 99 (75.8%) cases analyzed."
According to the authors the overall conclusion the main effects remained the same after stratifying by response to both of the blinding questions. However, the data are not provided.

5 manuscripts in the control group and 4 in the intervention group were lost to follow-up. However, this was before the intervention was delivered and was due to the authors not responding within the deadline. It is unlikely this would have affected the outcome in any meaningful way. After the reviews, 5 manuscripts were rejected in each group. It is unclear if the editors rejecting were blinded or not. However, it is unlikely that this would have had an effect on the result as the rejected manuscripts were of low quality.

The study was not preregistered. We asked the study authors for information on preplanned analyses, but they did not respond. Selective outcome reporting is not considered likely (as the results are disappointing), but cannot be ruled out.
No other sources of bias identified.

domains.domain_context_preview: ## Enrolment and Randomization Of the 327 originals received between May 2004 and March 2005, 196 (59.9%) were directly rejected by the editorial team. The remaining 131 (40.1%) were selected by the editorial committee as possible publications and therefore randomized. Of these, 2 were excluded either as a result of an administrative error (n = 1) or because the authors refused to participate (n = 1). From the 129 randomized manuscripts, 14 were lost of follow up because authors missed the deadline and the masked allocation was revealed; 21 (18.3%) of the 115 included papers were “interventions”, but only 3 were randomized clinical trials, 46 (40.0%) were longitudinal designs, 28 (24.3%) cross-sectional and 20 (17.4%) others. On the other hand, 16 were rejected by the editorial team after evaluating peer-review reports. The rejected papers had a significantly lower initial score on the...

domains.domain_context_preview: ## Assessment and Procedure Two evaluators (EC, RD) independently rated the reporting quality of manuscripts at initial submission and following peer review and revision, according to the MQAI. Both knew the initial and final status but were blinded to the intervention group. The final score awarded to each scale item was reached by averaging the two evaluators' item scores after allowing each one to modify his or her score once the reasons for the other evaluator's score was made known. Primary outcome was defined as the difference in the quality of papers between the initial and final submission, expressed as the sum of the 36 specific MQAI items, resulting in a minimum of 36 (lowest quality) and a maximum of 180 points. --- ## Enrolment and Randomization Of the 327 originals received between May 2004 and March 2005, 196 (59.9%) were directly rejected by the editorial team. The...

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Introduction Despite being widely accepted as the best way to filter low-quality research, to detect flaws in scientific communications and to improve papers with significant contributions to their fields [1]–[3], peer review has also raised many criticisms [4]–[6]. Certainly, as a process carried out by humans it has its weaknesses and therefore many initiatives have been developed to improve it. For instance, some methodological mistakes are continually repeated in published papers and proposals that referees fail to detect or do not consider properly, and because of this, both the development of reporting guidelines [7]–[11] and the suggestion of adding methodological experts to the referees panel have been promoted [12] and implemented [13]. Accordingly, from what has been said, we might have expected the existence of strong scientific evidence in favor of peer review, but...

domains.domain_context_preview: ## METHODS A repeated measures design was used to compare two groups of family caregivers of patients with schizophrenia: a psycho-education and support group and a standard care (conventional psychiatric outpatient care) group. This study was undertaken between January 2005 and September 2006. Analysis of data was on an intention-to-treat basis [35]. All subjects, who completed the intervention or not, were followed up over a 12-month post-intervention period. They were selected randomly from two regional outpatient clinics in Hong Kong, consisting of about 1,800 patients with schizophrenia or its subtypes, representing 12% of this type of patients in Hong Kong [36]. --- ## Models of Family Intervention in Schizophrenia Because of changes in the organization of mental health services in both Western and Asian countries [4,16], the past decade has witnessed a substantial increase in...

domains.domain_context_preview: ## Sample Characteristics Thirty-one subjects (91.2%) from the mutual psychoeducation and support group completed the program. These subjects, together with those who dropped out or absented in more than four group sessions - psycho-education group (n = 3) and routine care group (n = 2), were evaluated at three times of outcome measurements. Reasons for dropout or discontinuation from the group interventions were mainly: insufficient time to attend (n = 2), patient’s mental state worsened (n = 3), not interested (n = 2), and the only person taking care of patient (n = 2). --- ## Strengths and Limitations of the Study Strengths in the design of the study include: (a) randomized treatment allocation with intention-to-treat analysis; (b) clinic staff and an outcome assessor who were blind to the families’ allocation of groups; (c) use of a standardized treatment protocol and guideline for...

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Models of Family Intervention in Schizophrenia Because of changes in the organization of mental health services in both Western and Asian countries [4,16], the past decade has witnessed a substantial increase in demands for family interventions within the community as well as a rapid growth of a variety of family intervention models, which have been influenced by behavioral and cognitive psychological therapies [13]. All family intervention programs offer psycho-education and psychosocial support to family members, and some include the patient, although the theoretical orientation of these interventions varies considerably. domains.domain_context_preview: ## Study Design Internet access by patients began in 2008. We randomly selected patients for Internet access between January 2008 and December 2008. The inclusion criteria included the following: patients who had been registered in the GSS database for 2 years and who were diagnosed with either primary open angle glaucoma (POAG), normal tension glaucoma (NTG), or ocular hypertension (OH). The exclusion criteria included the following: patients who were under 20 years old at the time of registration; patients who had a history of intraocular surgery in both eyes; patients for whom accurate IOP measurement was deemed difficult; patients who had received oral glaucoma therapy, such as carbonic anhydrase inhibitors; patients who had a disease other than glaucoma that caused visual field defects; patients with a visual acuity of less than 20/60 or a mean deviation (MD) value lower than –20...

trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Introduction Glaucoma is one of the leading causes of acquired blindness and reduction of intraocular pressure (IOP) is the only proven therapy. Glaucoma treatment generally consists of antiglaucoma ophthalmic solutions—medications that are required for long periods of time [1]. Recent studies have revealed that a number of patients fail to comply with proper glaucoma medication regimens [2] because many glaucoma patients lack any glaucoma-related symptoms. --- ## Introduction Treatment adherence is a major concern in many chronic diseases [3], and improving patients’ understanding of their diseases has been found to be vital for appropriate treatment [4-6]. A variety of interventions have been used to increase understanding, including patient education during medical examinations, public lectures, distributing medical information pamphlets, and mass media advertising [7-11]. methodology.additional_notes: Trial registration number UMIN000006982. Written informed consent was obtained from all of the patients. CONSORT checklist referenced (Multimedia Appendix 1).

domain_rows.support_text: Comment: Randomization is stated but the method of sequence generation is not reported in the article excerpts or extracted methodology. No description of a random component (computer, random table, coin toss, etc.) was provided, so there is insufficient information to judge.

support_context.quote: This randomized, observer-blinded, prospective trial study was performed... In total, 194 patients were randomly assigned to the two groups...
support_context.relevance: States the trial was randomized and gives participant numbers but does not describe how the random sequence was generated.

support_context.quote: "randomization_method": "Not reported"
support_context.relevance: Explicitly indicates the sequence generation method was not reported in the extracted methods.

domain_rows.reasoning: The report declares random assignment but provides no detail on the sequence generation process. According to Cochrane criteria, absence of information about the random component means risk of bias for sequence generation is unclear.
ground_truth_support: Generation of random sequence not reported; only “we randomly selected” (p. 3)

domain_rows.support_text: Comment: The report states only that 194 patients were "randomly assigned" but provides no details on the method of sequence generation or how allocations were concealed from recruiters/enrollers. Because the report does not describe central randomization, sealed envelopes with safeguards, or other concealment procedures, there is insufficient information to determine whether allocation could be foreseen.

support_context.quote: In total, 194 patients were randomly assigned to the two groups, and 81 IA and 90 NIA patients completed the study.
support_context.relevance: Shows random assignment occurred but gives no details on concealment procedures.

support_context.quote: "randomization_method": "Not reported", "allocation_concealment_method": "Not reported"
support_context.relevance: Explicitly indicates allocation concealment method was not reported in the extracted methodology.

domain_rows.reasoning: There is no description of allocation concealment (central randomization, opaque sealed envelopes with safeguards, or equivalent). Under Cochrane criteria, absence of sufficient detail requires an 'Unclear risk' judgment rather than Low or High.
ground_truth_support: Allocation concealment not reported Comparison of baseline characteristics suggests that randomisation did not work: 63% males in the intervention group, 81% in the control group (p. 15, Table 1)

domain_rows.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel: All outcomes
domain_rows.support_text: Quote: "This randomized, observer-blinded, prospective trial study was performed in accordance with the Helsinki Treaty..." Comment: The report states the trial was "observer-blinded" but gives no information that participants or treating personnel were blinded; given the nature of the intervention (internet access vs no internet access), participants and personnel would have been aware of allocation and outcomes such as satisfaction, compliance and knowledge are subjective and likely influenced by lack of blinding. Probably not done.

support_context.quote: "This randomized, observer-blinded, prospective trial"
support_context.relevance: Indicates only observer blinding; does not claim participant/personnel blinding.

support_context.quote: outcomes... Internet access (IA) group were compared with... non–Internet access group (NIA)
support_context.relevance: Intervention (Internet access) is apparent to participants and staff, making blinding infeasible and outcomes subjective.

domain_rows.reasoning: The trial explicitly describes observer blinding but provides no indication participants or personnel were blinded. The interventions (internet access vs none) are obvious to participants, and key outcomes (satisfaction, compliance, knowledge) are subjective and likely influenced by awareness of allocation, producing high risk of performance bias.
ground_truth_support: Objective outcomes Blinding of patients not possible

domain_rows.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment: All outcomes
domain_rows.support_text: Quote: "This randomized, observer-blinded, prospective trial study was performed in accordance with the Helsinki Treaty and was approved by the University of Yamanashi Ethical Review Board." Comment: Observer-blinded indicates outcome assessors were blinded to group allocation; outcomes (IOP, visual field, objective measures) are unlikely to be influenced by assessor knowledge. Probably done.

support_context.quote: This randomized, observer-blinded, prospective trial study was performed in accordance with the Helsinki Treaty...
support_context.relevance: States that observers (outcome assessors) were blinded, directly relevant to detection bias.

support_context.quote: patients... mean deviation (MD) value ... determined by the Humphrey Field Analyzer (HFA) central 30-2 program
support_context.relevance: Key outcomes measured by objective instruments (HFA), reducing risk of assessment bias.

domain_rows.reasoning: The report explicitly states the trial was observer-blinded and primary outcomes were instrument-based (e.g., IOP, Humphrey Field Analyzer MD), so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcome measurement, supporting Low risk.
ground_truth_support: Objective outcomes Unblinding of physicians who measure ophthalmologic outcomes is easily possible. No investigation whether blinding has successfully been maintained was performed

domain_rows.support_text: Quote: "In total, 194 patients were randomly assigned to the two groups, and 81 IA and 90 NIA patients completed the study." Comment: Numbers completing are reported and the text states "no significant differences were found in the reasons for loss to follow up between the two groups," suggesting missing outcome data were balanced with similar reasons across groups. This meets criteria for low risk.

support_context.quote: 194 patients were randomly assigned...81 IA and 90 NIA patients completed the study.
support_context.relevance: Shows the amount of missing data by group and numbers completing the study.

support_context.quote: no significant differences were found in the reasons for loss to follow up between the two groups.
support_context.relevance: Indicates reasons for missing data were similar across groups, reducing risk of attrition bias.

domain_rows.reasoning: The trial reports the numbers randomized and completed by group and explicitly states reasons for loss to follow-up did not differ between groups; missing data appear balanced and unlikely related to outcomes, so risk of bias from incomplete outcome data is low.
ground_truth_support: Objective outcomes Follow‐up: 92% and 94% have been analysed (p. 11, Figure 1)
Patient‐reported outcomes No patient‐reported outcomes
